prior.title: New Jersey Department of Transportation Freight Planning Framework and Resources
prior.summary: NJDOT's comprehensive freight planning portal providing oversight of multimodal freight distribution (highway, rail, water, aviation) across New Jersey. The department manages statewide freight plans (2023, 2017, 2007), freight management systems, performance measures, and regulatory enforcement for the trucking industry. Includes initiatives such as goods movement action plans, southern NJ port assessment, and truck monitoring programs.
prior.full_brief:
# New Jersey Freight Planning Framework and Resources

## Regulatory Intelligence Brief — Market Signal / Framework Profile

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document profiles the New Jersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT) freight planning framework as it stands in 2023–2026, including its federal funding dependencies, multimodal scope, regional planning architecture, and the federal guidance context that governs its obligations. It is written for workspaces operating freight forwarding, air cargo, road drayage, and port-adjacent logistics in the New York–New Jersey–Philadelphia corridor.

**Convention notes:** Binding-obligation language from federal statutes is quoted verbatim and cited. All analytical and interpretive statements are labeled. Legal conclusions are routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts. The Federal Register source (https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees) returned a CAPTCHA block during retrieval; no content from that source could be extracted. Claims attributable only to that source are recorded as explicit gaps.

**Severity: MONITORING** — The 2023 SFP is in force and implementation is active. The next four-year update cycle creates a planning horizon through approximately 2027. No immediate compliance action falls on private freight operators; the document is a strategic positioning and corridor-intelligence instrument.

---

# 2. What This Framework Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**FACT:** "The New Jersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT) is responsible for freight planning activities encompassing all modes of freight distribution: highway, rail, water and aviation." *Source: NJDOT Freight Planning Overview, nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan, State of New Jersey, accessed 2026.*

**FACT:** "The 2023 Statewide Freight Plan (SFP) is a multimodal plan that reflects the state's diverse freight transportation needs and demands, given that most cargo relies on two or more modes before arriving in stores or on your doorstep." *Source: NJDOT 2023 Statewide Freight Plan page, www.nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan/stateplan23.shtm, State of New Jersey, August 2023.*

**FACT:** "New Jersey's geographic location positions it as a critical link in the national freight network and provides direct access to the major metropolitan markets of New York City and Philadelphia, serving as a gateway to global markets." *Source: NJDOT 2023 Statewide Freight Plan page, www.nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan/stateplan23.shtm, State of New Jersey, August 2023.*

**FACT:** "Coupled with New Jersey's location is its extensive multimodal freight network that includes highways, rail lines, air routes, marine waterways, and pipelines." *Source: NJDOT 2023 Statewide Freight Plan page, www.nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan/stateplan23.shtm, State of New Jersey, August 2023.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with air cargo operations centered on Newark Liberty International Airport, road drayage into the Port of New York and New Jersey, or transloading through northern and central New Jersey warehouse corridors, the NJDOT SFP is the primary state-level planning document shaping infrastructure investment, permit policy, and corridor priority over the next four years. Understanding its priorities positions the workspace ahead of competitors on route planning, capital program timing, and grant eligibility conversations with shipper clients.

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — No immediate private-operator compliance deadline.** The NJDOT SFP does not impose direct obligations on freight forwarders or cargo operators. Its significance is strategic: it determines where state and federal capital flows, which corridors receive infrastructure upgrades, and which freight modes are prioritized in the STIP programming cycle through FY2029.

*Operational implication:* Workspaces should treat the SFP's four-year implementation window (2023–2027) as the planning horizon for corridor capacity and infrastructure changes in the New York–New Jersey gateway. Decisions on facility location, carrier contract terms, and drayage routing that extend beyond 2026 should be stress-tested against the SFP's stated challenges and investment priorities.

**Anticipated authoritative guidance gap — February 2026 Federal Register guidance:** The Federal Register document at https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees was listed as a corroborating source governing state freight plan obligations under FAST Act/IIJA. The source returned a CAPTCHA access block; no content was extractable. *Specific regulatory content of the February 2026 FHWA guidance on multimodal state freight plans not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-10.* Workspaces with compliance interest in how federal guidance shapes NJDOT's next plan update should retrieve this document directly via the Federal Register API or FHWA policy contacts.

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Planning and Funding Chain

**FACT:** "While the Statewide Freight Plan became a required element passed as part of FAST (Fixing America's Surface Transportation) Act Legislation, the IIJA (Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act) updated its freight planning requirements so that NJDOT must update its Statewide Freight Plan once every four years." *Source: NJDOT 2023 Statewide Freight Plan page, www.nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan/stateplan23.shtm, State of New Jersey, August 2023.*

**FACT:** "This requirement is directly tied to the state receiving its National Highway Freight Program funding (currently $402M through FY2029)." *Source: NJDOT 2023 Statewide Freight Plan page, www.nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan/stateplan23.shtm, State of New Jersey, August 2023.*

**FACT:** The FHWA fact sheet states that "Beginning on December 4, 2017 (two years after enactment of the FAST Act) a State may not obligate NHFP funds unless it has developed a freight plan that is consistent with 49 U.S.C. 70202." *Source: FHWA National Highway Freight Program FAST Act Fact Sheet, www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm, FHWA/USDOT, updated August 2024.*

**FACT:** The NHFP fact sheet states that "A State may transfer to the National Highway Performance Program, Surface Transportation Block Grant Program, Transportation Alternatives, Highway Safety Improvement Program, and Congestion Mitigation and Air Quality Improvement Program up to 50% of NHFP funds made available each fiscal year." *Source: FHWA National Highway Freight Program FAST Act Fact Sheet, www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm, FHWA/USDOT, updated August 2024.*

*Analytical inference:* The $402M NHFP funding envelope through FY2029 is the primary capital mechanism through which NJDOT will fund highway freight infrastructure improvements in this planning cycle. For workspaces dependent on specific highway corridors or intermodal connectors, projects funded under this envelope will determine near-term capacity and congestion conditions on those routes.

*Analytical inference:* The SFP's role as a prerequisite for NHFP fund obligation creates a structural incentive for NJDOT to keep its plan current and its freight investment plan aligned with federal program requirements. This makes the plan's stated priorities more likely to materialize into funded projects than aspirational state plans without this funding lock.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether private freight operators, freight forwarders, or intermodal logistics providers have standing to formally participate in NJDOT's freight plan update process, and what legal weight NJDOT's SFP carries for permit or regulatory decisions affecting private operators, requires legal review.

---

# 5. Substantive Framework Content — Multimodal Scope and Infrastructure Picture

## 5.1 Port of New York and New Jersey

**FACT:** "Northern New Jersey is the home of the largest container port on the Atlantic seaboard. The Port handled 8.7 million TEUs in 2024. The rail moves inland at the Port totaled 639,657 in 2024. The Port also handled 407,657 vehicles, 2.68 million metric tons of bulk cargo, and 845,532 cruise passengers during 2024." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, North Jersey Transportation Planning Authority, 2026. Underlying data sourced by NJTPA from Port Authority of New York and New Jersey.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces managing ocean import or transloading activity through Port Newark/Elizabeth, the 8.7 million TEU 2024 volume is the baseline against which port infrastructure investment and congestion planning in the NJDOT SFP is calibrated. Port-adjacent drayage and warehouse clients should understand that NJDOT capital programming is responding to this throughput level.

## 5.2 Rail

**FACT:** "Northern New Jersey is served by two Class I railroads—CSX and Norfolk Southern, a regional railroad (the New York, Susquehanna & Western), and several short line railroads. This region also includes the Conrail Shared Assets Area and extensive on- and near-dock rail operations that serve the Port." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

**FACT:** "In 2023, New Jersey had 17.2 million tons of rail freight originating in the State and 21.6 million tons terminating in the State." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026. Underlying data sourced by NJTPA from the Association of American Railroads.*

*Operational implication:* The terminating-exceeds-originating pattern (21.6M vs. 17.2M tons) confirms New Jersey's structural role as a destination-heavy distribution state rather than an export-origination hub, which has direct implications for how workspaces structure inbound vs. outbound rail capacity agreements for clients receiving goods in the region.

## 5.3 Air Cargo

**FACT:** "During 2023, Newark Liberty International Airport handled nearly 686,700 tons of air cargo. In 2023, the three leading airlines in terms of cargo volume were Federal Express, United and UPS. In 2022, the Airport was ranked as the 12th largest air cargo airport in North America and 39th in the World." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026. Underlying data sourced by NJTPA from Port Authority of NY and NJ.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with air-primary transport modes, Newark Liberty's position as the 12th largest air cargo airport in North America and its FedEx/UPS/United dominance is the competitive baseline. Workspace positioning in the Newark air cargo market operates against this carrier concentration. Capacity constraints or infrastructure investments at EWR flagged in NJDOT's SFP will affect slot availability and ground handling economics.

## 5.4 Trucking

**FACT:** "Trucking moves the most freight in the NJTPA region. Approximately 70 percent of the total 399 million tons of goods moved in the region were handled by trucks." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026. Underlying data sourced by NJTPA from NJTPA 2050 Freight Industry Level Forecasts Update.*

*Operational implication:* A 70% trucking modal share across 399 million tons establishes road drayage as the dominant last-mile mechanism in this region. NJDOT SFP investments in truck-relevant infrastructure — grade separations, interchange geometry, truck parking — have direct and near-term operating cost implications for workspaces managing drayage-heavy supply chains in the region.

## 5.5 Industrial Property Base

**FACT:** "As of the second quarter of 2025, northern and central New Jersey had 875.5 million square feet of industrial properties, one of the leading concentrations in North America. About 2.3 million square feet of new construction started during the quarter. Vacancies continued to increase during the quarter and are now at 7.2%." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026. Underlying data sourced by NJTPA from CBRE.*

*Analytical inference:* Rising industrial vacancy (7.2% as of Q2 2025) alongside continuing new construction suggests a supply overhang in the northern and central New Jersey logistics real estate market. For workspaces advising clients on warehouse or fulfillment network decisions in this region, this vacancy level may create near-term lease negotiation leverage, though new supply continues to enter the market.

---

# 6. NJDOT Planning Architecture — Programs, Tools, and Regional Partners

## 6.1 NJDOT Freight Planning Initiatives

**FACT:** NJDOT's freight planning framework includes the following active initiatives listed on its freight planning overview page: the Freight Management System, Freight Performance Measures, Goods Movement Action Plan, Southern NJ Freight Transportation and Economic Development Assessment, Southern NJ Port Complex, and Truck Monitoring Program. *Source: NJDOT Freight Planning Overview, nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan, State of New Jersey, accessed 2026.*

**FACT:** "NJDOT is responsible for issuance of route specific oversize/overweight truck permits using Superload, an online web-based routing system. NJDOT is also responsible for regulatory and enforcement matters related to the trucking industry and serves as the lead agency for the Motor Carrier Safety Assistance Program (MCSAP) sponsored by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA)." *Source: NJDOT Freight Planning Overview, nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan, State of New Jersey, accessed 2026.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces managing oversize or overweight cargo movements — relevant to high-value automotive, live event staging equipment, or film/TV production freight — NJDOT's Superload system is the direct operational interface for New Jersey routing. Familiarity with this system and its routing constraints is a practical operational requirement, not a planning abstraction.

**FACT:** "The Department also collaborates with other transportation agencies on studies, projects and other public and private initiatives, such as the State Rail Plan with NJ TRANSIT." *Source: NJDOT Freight Planning Overview, nj.gov/transportation/freight/plan, State of New Jersey, accessed 2026.*

## 6.2 Regional MPO Partner — NJTPA

**FACT:** The NJTPA "supports freight in the region by: Identifying and addressing regional and local freight needs in coordination with public and private sector partners; Developing and disseminating freight data, forecasts, and planning tools; Conducting freight planning studies; Managing the Freight Concept Development Program to advance local and regional freight initiatives; Hosting the Freight Initiatives Committee to serve as a regional forum for freight dialogue and education." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

**FACT:** The Freight Activity Locator is described as "NJTPA's ArcGIS-based freight data hub for North Jersey, providing interactive maps, dashboards, and datasets on freight facilities, corridors, truck parking, and commodity, and growth forecasts." The tool includes "themed pages for the regional freight systems, developments opportunities, truck parking, and freight profiles" and "2050 freight industry level forecasts by region and by commodity type, including data on highway utilization, e-commerce, freight facilities, tonnage and more." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

*Operational implication:* The Freight Activity Locator is a publicly available competitive intelligence tool. Workspaces can use it to screen corridors and facilities for freight significance, map evidence of freight activity for grant justification purposes, and identify truck parking constraints — all without proprietary data acquisition costs.

**FACT:** The NJTPA states that data from the Freight Activity Locator may be used to "Screen corridors and sites for freight significance; Justify grants and investments with mapped evidence of freight activity and needs; Understand how local industrial/warehouse districts fit into the regional system; Identify truck parking constraints and high-impact freight opportunity areas." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

## 6.3 Freight Initiatives Committee

**FACT:** "The Freight Initiatives Committee (FIC) hosts public bi-monthly meetings to discuss regional updates, provide learning opportunities and provides a forum to discuss regional freight trends." Participation is open and notices are available through the NJTPA E-list. *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces seeking early intelligence on regional freight infrastructure decisions, carrier behavior, and planning studies before they become published documents, participation in the FIC bi-monthly meetings is a low-cost forward-intelligence channel. Industry representatives present at FIC meetings; workspace participation creates a standing presence in the regional planning dialogue.

## 6.4 Freight Concept Development Program

**FACT:** "The NJTPA established the Freight Concept Development Program (FCDP) to advance regional and local freight initiatives identified through planning studies conducted by the NJTPA, its planning partners and its subregions. The FCDP is consistent with federal guidelines and the Project Delivery Process implemented by the New Jersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT). Moving forward the NJTPA plans to conduct FCDP studies every three years." *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

**FACT:** Active or recently completed FCDP studies include: Dover and Rockaway Rail Realignment Project; Hackettstown Bridge over Drain Weight Restriction Elimination Project; Berkshire Valley Road Truck Circulation Project in Roxbury; Port Reading Secondary South Main Street Grade Crossing Elimination Project; Southern Middlesex County North-South Truck Corridor Project in Cranbury and Monroe (ongoing); East Hanover Avenue Bridge Catenary Rail Clearance Project in Morris Plains and Morris Township (ongoing). *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

---

# 7. NHFP Funding Framework — Federal Rules Governing NJDOT's Capital Access

## 7.1 Program Purpose

**FACT:** The FHWA NHFP fact sheet states the program's purpose includes "investing in infrastructure and operational improvements that strengthen economic competitiveness, reduce congestion, reduce the cost of freight transportation, improve reliability, and increase productivity; improving the safety, security, efficiency, and resiliency of freight transportation in rural and urban areas; improving the state of good repair of the NHFN." [23 U.S.C. 167 (a), (b)]. *Source: FHWA NHFP FAST Act Fact Sheet, www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm, FHWA/USDOT, updated August 2024.*

## 7.2 Eligible Activities Relevant to Workspace Operations

**FACT:** NHFP-eligible activities include: "Railway-highway grade separation; Geometric improvements to interchanges and ramps; Truck-only lanes; Climbing and runaway truck lanes; Adding or widening of shoulders; Truck parking facilities eligible for funding under section 1401 (Jason's Law) of MAP-21; Real-time traffic, truck parking, roadway condition, and multimodal transportation information systems; Electronic screening and credentialing systems for vehicles, including weigh-in-motion truck inspection technologies; Traffic signal optimization, including synchronized and adaptive signals." [23 U.S.C. 167(i)(5)(C)]. *Source: FHWA NHFP FAST Act Fact Sheet, www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm, FHWA/USDOT, updated August 2024.*

*Analytical inference:* Projects funded under these eligible categories — grade separations, interchange improvements, truck parking — directly affect drayage cycle times and routing reliability for workspaces operating road freight in northern and central New Jersey. When NJDOT's SFP freight investment plan identifies specific projects in these categories, those projects signal where infrastructure capacity improvements are coming and on what timeline.

## 7.3 State Freight Plan — Performance Accountability

**FACT:** "If FHWA determines that a State has not met (or made significant progress toward meeting) its 23 U.S.C 150 performance targets related to freight movement by the date that is 2 years after the performance targets are established, the State must submit to FHWA a report that describes the actions it will take to meet these targets." [23 U.S.C. 167(j)]. *Source: FHWA NHFP FAST Act Fact Sheet, www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm, FHWA/USDOT, updated August 2024.*

*Analytical inference:* The FHWA performance accountability mechanism creates an incentive for NJDOT to demonstrate measurable freight performance progress, not merely plan publication. For workspaces, this means the SFP is more than a planning document — it is a performance commitment that shapes NJDOT's operational and capital priorities under federal scrutiny.

---

# 8. Registration and Reporting Obligations

*No content for this section as of 2026-06-10: The NJDOT freight planning framework does not impose registration or reporting obligations directly on freight forwarders, cargo operators, or logistics providers. NJDOT's MCSAP role (motor carrier safety enforcement) and Superload permit system are the operative regulatory interfaces for trucking operators; these are operational requirements rather than framework-level reporting obligations.*

---

# 9. Competitive Positioning Implications

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that actively track NJDOT's SFP implementation — including which projects advance from the freight investment plan into STIP programming — gain lead time on corridor capacity changes before competitors who rely on reactive market intelligence. The NJTPA's Transportation Improvement Program (TIP) lists active freight projects that are already funded and scheduled, creating a publicly accessible forward-infrastructure map.

**FACT:** Active freight projects in the NJTPA TIP include: Port Street Corridor Improvement Project (Project ID: PA2201); Delancy Street, Avenue I to Avenue P in Essex County (Project ID: NS0504); Kapkowski Road – North Avenue East Improvement Project (Project ID: 17339); Portway, Fish House Road/Pennsylvania Avenue, CR 659 in Hudson County (Project ID: 97005B); Local Freight Impact Fund Program (Project ID: 17390); Maritime Transportation System (Project ID: 01309); New Jersey Rail Freight Assistance Program (Project ID: X34). *Source: NJTPA Freight Planning Hub, www.njtpa.org/Planning/Regional-Programs/Freight.aspx, NJTPA, 2026.*

*Analytical inference:* Competitors with established relationships in the NJDOT planning process — through the Freight Initiatives Committee or FCDP — have earlier visibility into which corridor investments are advancing and which are stalled. Workspaces currently without this presence are at an informational disadvantage on route and facility planning decisions with multi-year lead times.

*Analytical inference:* The Goods Movement Community Engagement Tool described by NJTPA — covering noise, safety, congestion, emissions, and community impacts of truck movement — signals that truck routing in dense residential corridors is an active regulatory pressure point in this region. Workspaces operating high-frequency drayage through residential or urban corridors in northern New Jersey should anticipate that this tool is being used by municipalities to identify and challenge truck routing, which could affect operational flexibility.

---

# 10. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- New Jersey's freight infrastructure plan is federally funded at $402M through FY2029, giving it more capital certainty than aspirational state plans. Infrastructure improvements in this window are more likely to materialize than typical state plans.
- Newark Liberty handled nearly 686,700 tons of air cargo in 2023, making it the 12th largest air cargo airport in North America — a credible claim that supports conversations with clients considering gateway selection.
- The Port of New York and New Jersey processed 8.7 million TEUs in 2024, the largest container port on the Atlantic seaboard — directly relevant to ocean import clients evaluating East Coast port options.
- Trucking accounts for 70% of regional freight by tonnage; rail provides inland connectivity from the port, with 639,657 rail moves in 2024. These figures support modal-split conversations with clients.
- Industrial vacancy in northern/central New Jersey is at 7.2% as of Q2 2025, with new supply still entering. This is a factual and credible data point for warehouse location advisory discussions.

**What to avoid claiming:**
- Do not assert that the NJDOT SFP guarantees specific project delivery timelines without checking the current STIP status of specific projects — plan inclusion and STIP programming are separate steps.
- Do not cite the February 2026 Federal Register guidance on state freight plans without first retrieving and reading it; its content was not accessible for this brief.
- Do not characterize NJTPA freight data as NJDOT data — NJTPA is the regional MPO for northern New Jersey, not the state DOT; the two organizations are partners, not the same entity.

---

# 11. Confirmed Planning and Funding Timeline

| Milestone | Date / Status | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 2007 Comprehensive Statewide Freight Plan — first in state history | 2007, in force as of that date | NJDOT Freight Planning Overview |
| FAST Act enacted — state freight plan becomes federal requirement | December 4, 2015 | FHWA NHFP Fact Sheet |
| NHFP fund obligation conditioned on state freight plan compliance | December 4, 2017 (two years post-FAST Act) | FHWA NHFP Fact Sheet |
| 2017 Statewide Freight Plan — released and FHWA-approved under FAST Act | 2017, in force | NJDOT Freight Planning Overview |
| IIJA enacted — four-year update cycle established | November 2021 | NJDOT 2023 SFP page |
| 2023 Statewide Freight Plan — published and federally acknowledged | 2023; USDOT listing updated April 10, 2024 | USDOT federal recognition page |
| NHFP funding envelope for NJ — $402M through FY2029 | Current, tied to SFP compliance | NJDOT 2023 SFP page |
| Next SFP update due (four-year cycle from 2023) | *Analytical inference:* approximately 2027 — no specific date published | IIJA update mandate |
| February 2026 Federal Register guidance on multimodal state freight plans | February 24, 2026 — content not extractable; see gap note in Section 3 | Federal Register listing |

---

# 12. Monitoring Window

**Severity: MONITORING.** Re-check window: **Q1 2027** — the four-year IIJA update cycle from the 2023 SFP publication sets the next plan update as approximately 2027. NJDOT will initiate stakeholder engagement and data collection for the next SFP update in advance of that deadline. Workspaces should monitor NJDOT and NJTPA announcements for the launch of the next SFP update process, which will include public participation opportunities and may surface new corridor priorities, investment plans, and performance targets that affect routing and facility decisions.

*Analytical inference:* The February 2026 Federal Register guidance on state freight plans may reset or refine the requirements NJDOT must meet in its next SFP update. Retrieval of that document (see gap note in Section 3) should occur before Q3 2026 to allow the workspace to track any changes to the planning framework that affect corridor investment priorities or stakeholder participation mechanisms.

---
